title: Testing User Flows
intro: Unit tests check individual functions, but real users don't call functions — they click buttons, fill forms, and navigate pages. Just like a theater needs a full dress rehearsal and not just line checks, your app needs end-to-end flow tests. Let's test actual user journeys through your task manager.

teach.title: What Is a User Flow Test?
teach.body: A user flow test simulates what a real user does: open the app, log in, create a task, mark it complete. These are integration or E2E tests that verify multiple components work together. They catch bugs that unit tests miss, like a form that submits but doesn't actually save to the database.

teach.title: User Flow Tests Are Like a Dress Rehearsal
teach.body: A theater doesn't check only that each actor knows their lines (unit tests). They run a full dress rehearsal with costumes, lighting, and scene changes (flow tests). Only by running the whole show together can you catch problems like a missed entrance or a prop that's not in the right place.

teach.title: A User Flow Test
teach.annotation: This test covers the complete user journey — login, create, and complete — catching integration issues that unit tests would miss.

teach.body: The user flows you test are the same ones that bring in revenue — sign up, use the product, come back. Making sure those paths work flawlessly is how you build a product people are willing to pay for.
Test the critical user flows end-to-end: sign up, create a task, complete a task. If these break, your app is fundamentally broken. Everything else is secondary.

start.narrative: You're writing user flow tests for the task manager. The product manager says the most important flow is: User logs in, creates a task, sets a due date, and completes it. How do you approach this?

choices.text: Write one massive E2E test covering login through task completion
Break it into focused integration tests: one for login, one for task creation, one for task completion
Only write unit tests for each component since they're faster

one-big-test.narrative: Your E2E test works but takes 30 seconds to run and breaks whenever the UI changes. When it fails, the error says 'element not found' but you don't know which step failed. You add descriptive labels and split into smaller tests. Now you need to pick a test runner before going further.

choices.text: Use Playwright with traces — every failure ships with a screenshot, DOM snapshot, and timeline
Stick with bare Node assertions and parse failures from console logs

balanced-approach.narrative: Your tests are clean: login is tested separately, task creation uses a pre-authenticated state, and completion builds on a pre-created task. But the PM asks: 'How do I know the full flow works if it's split up?' What do you say?

choices.text: Add one smoke test that runs the full flow end-to-end, while keeping the detailed tests separate
The integration tests cover each piece — if all pieces work, the flow works

unit-only.narrative: All unit tests pass. But when a user tries to create a task, the form submits successfully yet no task appears in the list. The API and frontend are both correct in isolation — the bug is in how they communicate. You agree to add real flow tests now.

choices.text: Add integration tests that drive the UI and check the database — same path the bug took
Mock the API in the integration tests so they stay fast

pick-selectors.narrative: Now you start writing the actual flow. The first step needs to click the 'New Task' button. How do you locate it in the test?

choices.text: Use a role-based selector: getByRole('button', { name: 'New Task' }) — survives styling and class changes
Add data-testid='new-task-btn' to the component and target that — explicit contract for tests
Use a brittle CSS selector like .header > div:nth-child(3) > button.primary

wait-strategy.narrative: Your test fills the form and clicks Save, then immediately checks the task list. It passes locally but fails in CI 1 in 5 runs — sometimes the new task isn't in the DOM yet. How do you handle the timing?

choices.text: Use the framework's auto-waiting assertions: expect(taskList).toContainText('Buy milk') retries until it appears or times out
Add await sleep(1000) before the assertion — fast enough, problem solved
Write a custom polling loop with setInterval

ci-integration.narrative: Your flow test is solid locally. Now you need it running on every pull request so a regression can never sneak in. How do you wire it into CI?

choices.text: Run the flow test on every PR and block merges on failure — with traces uploaded as artifacts so failures are debuggable
Run it nightly on main only — keeps PRs fast
Leave it as a local-only test — devs can run it before merging if they want

excellent-end.narrative: Your task manager has a focused flow test stack: role-based selectors, auto-waiting assertions, and a smoke test that runs on every PR. When a regression slips into a styling refactor next quarter, the failed PR check catches it before any user ever sees the broken flow.
excellent-end.feedback: This is the full picture of user flow testing. Stable selectors, auto-waiting assertions, and CI gating turn flow tests into a true safety net — not just a thing devs run when they remember to.

mediocre-end.narrative: Your flow tests catch issues, but only the morning after a regression merges. By the time the nightly fails, three more PRs have stacked on top and you have to bisect.
mediocre-end.feedback: Nightly runs are better than nothing, but flow tests pay off most when they block bad code from merging. Move them to PR-blocking once they're stable.

weak-end.narrative: The flow test exists but nobody runs it. Three weeks in, it stops passing because nobody noticed an API endpoint moved. The test rots and a real user reports the broken signup flow.
weak-end.feedback: An untested test is worse than no test — it gives false confidence that 'we have flow coverage.' If you can't put it in CI, delete it; if it's worth keeping, make it block merges.
